text.title: Vector DB Index Tuning — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules: bench before tune, quantize with recall floor, workload-driven M, persist tuning record, recall regression gate.

rule.statement: Index tuning MUST start with a baseline benchmark on the current production config. Tuning without a baseline is forbidden — gains have no reference.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, "improvement" is unmeasurable. Document recall, p95, memory at defaults; every subsequent change cites the baseline.

rule.statement: Quantization (scalar / product / binary) MUST be applied only if measured recall stays ≥ recall_floor in the SLA. Skipping the recall check before quantization is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Aggressive quantization (PQ, binary) lifts speed/memory but degrades recall 3-10%. Verify each step before adopting.

rule.statement: HNSW `M` MUST be set by workload class: read-heavy {32-64}, balanced {16-32}, write-heavy {16}. Single global M for mixed workloads is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Larger M improves query quality but slows insert + bloats memory. Each workload class has a different sweet spot.

rule.statement: Every tuning iteration MUST be recorded in a `tuning-history.yaml` with timestamp + params + measured (recall, latency, memory). Tribal-knowledge tuning is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Without history, the team re-debates the same tuning options every quarter. Recorded history compresses decision-making.

rule.statement: After tuning, re-bench MUST run against the same query workload. If recall regresses below SLA floor, rollback is mandatory. No exceptions.
rule.rationale: Aggressive tuning can land within latency SLA while crashing recall below acceptable. Gate is the only safe roll-forward.
